title: Approximate quantum error correction theory of non-isometric codes
authors: Yixu Wang, Yijia Xu, Zi-Wen Liu
abstract: Non-isometric encoding arises in various important contexts in quantum error correction, most notably in the finite-energy, non-ideal codewords inevitable in experimental realizations of continuous-variable codes, and holographic quantum gravity. In this work, we present a general and systematic theory of non-isometric quantum error-correcting codes. In particular, we employ the approximate quantum error correction framework to quantitatively study the fundamental limitations imposed by non-isometric encodings on the accuracy of quantum error correction and implementation of logical operations. We apply our theory to analyze GKP and tiger codes under energy constraints, and discuss the implications to holography.
summary: This paper establishes a comprehensive, systematic theory for quantum error correction codes operating under non-isometric encoding conditions. It quantifies the fundamental limits on achievable fidelity and logical operation accuracy using information-theoretic measures derived from the code's Gram operator. This advances QEC theory by bridging abstract mathematics with physical constraints found in continuous-variable systems and quantum gravity.
main_problem: To develop a general theory for quantum error correction (QEC) that accounts for non-isometric encoding, which arises in realistic physical implementations.
main_result: The paper derives explicit formulas and bounds for intrinsic optimal recovery fidelities ($F_{\min}, F^2_{	ext{avg}}, F_{	ext{Choi}}$) based on the condition ratio of the Gram operator $V^\dagger V$. It also establishes necessary conditions for noise resilience in non-isometric settings.
method: The analysis employs information-theoretic measures (fidelity, purified distance) and utilizes mathematical tools like eigenvalue analysis, random matrix theory (Marchenko–Pastur distribution), and optimization techniques based on quasiconvexity.
model_or_system: The theoretical framework models quantum encoding via a map $V: \mathcal{H}_L ightarrow \mathcal{H}_P$. Specific examples include GKP codes and Tiger codes, which are analyzed in the context of continuous-variable systems and holographic gravity (AdS/CFT).
key_observables: Channel fidelity ($F_ho$), purified distance ($\epsilon_ho$), Condition Ratio ($r$), and various optimal recovery fidelities ($F_{\min}, F^2_{	ext{avg}}, F_{	ext{Choi}}$).
important_parameters: The condition ratio $r = \lambda_{\min} / \lambda_{\max}$ of the Gram operator $V^\dagger V$; dimension ratios like $d_L/d_P$ in random matrix limits.
main_assumptions: The primary assumption is moving beyond the standard isometric encoding assumption. In some analyses, it assumes $ orall i, \lambda_i > 0$ to avoid trivial kernels.
figures_summary: Not specified (No figure descriptions were extracted).
paper_structure: The paper systematically builds theory by first defining non-isometric encodings and deriving intrinsic fidelity limits using the condition ratio. It then applies this framework to specific codes (GKP, Tiger) and physical contexts (holography), culminating in establishing general inequalities between different fidelity measures.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant as it provides a rigorous mathematical toolset for analyzing practical limitations in quantum computation that arise from imperfect hardware or fundamental physics models like holography.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
